description.en: The Philippines' breakfast bread, born from Spanish 'pan de sal' and gone sweeter and softer over centuries: an egg-and-milk-enriched roll rolled in breadcrumbs before baking, best torn open steaming hot and dunked in coffee.
description.de: Das philippinische Frühstücksbrötchen, entstanden aus dem spanischen 'pan de sal' und über Jahrhunderte süßer und weicher geworden: ein mit Ei und Milch angereichertes Brötchen, vor dem Backen in Semmelbröseln gewälzt, dampfend heiß in Kaffee getunkt.
description.es: El pan de desayuno de Filipinas, nacido del 'pan de sal' español y vuelto más dulce y suave con los siglos: un panecillo enriquecido con huevo y leche, rebozado en pan rallado antes de hornear, mejor abierto humeante y mojado en café.
description.fr: Le pain du petit-déjeuner aux Philippines, né du 'pan de sal' espagnol et devenu plus doux et moelleux au fil des siècles : un petit pain enrichi à l'œuf et au lait, roulé dans la chapelure avant cuisson, meilleur déchiré fumant et trempé dans le café.

origin.note: Descended from the Spanish 'pan de sal' (salt bread) introduced during colonization; the name stuck even though the modern roll turned notably sweet rather than salty.

ingredients.note: for rolling the shaped rolls before baking

keeping.note: Best warm the morning it's baked, often dunked in coffee or filled with corned beef; reheats well from frozen.

summary.en: Combine both flours, sugar, salt and yeast, then work in warm milk and eggs to a rough dough.
Knead 8-10 minutes to a strong gluten structure, then work in softened butter a little at a time.
Let the dough double, about an hour, then gently deflate it.
Roll into logs, cut into portions, and shape each into a smooth ball or short log.
Roll each piece in dried breadcrumbs and set it on a breadcrumb-dusted tray.
Cover and let rise again until doubled, about an hour.
Bake until golden on top, soft and light inside.

source.adaptation: Grams kept as sourced; the two flour types combined into a single ingredient entry.
